Watkinson /Herkenne /Guy /Hadgraft / Oliveira /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 16 where J represents drug flux, D is the drug diffusion co- efficient, K is the partition coefficient for drug between the stratum corneum and the vehicle, C vehicle is the drug concentration in the vehicle and h is the diffusional path length for the drug. If a cosolvent does not affect membrane barrier prop- erties, its inclusion in the formulation of a weakly acidic drug of the form HX is expected to (1) increase the unionized fraction (HX) of the drug. Since the latter has a higher lipophilicity relative to X – , the overall skin flux, which is proportional to the skin-formulation partition coefficient, K (equation 1) should theoretically be enhanced ; (2) increase drug solubility in the formulation. If the drug concentration is kept constant, this effectively de- creases the degree of saturation and hence should lead to a lower flux. The precise outcome will depend ulti- mately on the nature of the cosolvent, the pH of the formulation and the pK a of the drug. Many non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are or- ganic acids with pK a s ranging from 3 to 5. At the pH of the skin surface (  5), such compounds will be ionized. In the deeper layers of the skin, a less acidic environment is encountered and the degree of ionization of such mol- ecules will therefore increase with consequent effects on solubility and permeation. Ibuprofen is one of the most widely available non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for the management of inflammation and is available in both gel and cream formulations. Although the perme- ation of ibuprofen has been studied from a range of ve- hicles [2] , there has been, to our knowledge, no system- atic investigation into the manner in which formulation components such as cosolvents may influence the ioniza- tion and permeability of ibuprofen into and through the skin. The aim of the present study therefore was to exam- ine the influence of increasing amounts of the cosolvent ethanol on the solubility, ionization and permeation characteristics of ibuprofen through silicone and human skin. As saturated solutions were used throughout this work, a further aim was to investigate the possibility of self-association of ibuprofen in the different cosolvent mixtures. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health- care, Havant, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility of Ibuprofen in Aqueous-Ethanol Mixtures Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were then centrifuged for 15 min at 4,000 g and the supernatant was diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was employed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml  min –1 . The retention time was approximate- ly 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis, and CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Saturated Aqueous Solubility as a Function of pH Excess drug was added to 10 ml of phosphate-citrate buffer [3] at different pH values and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath maintained at 32 ° C. The ionic strength was con- trolled by inclusion of 3 M NaClO 4 in the buffer solutions. Solu- tions were centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was diluted and assayed by UV spectroscopy. Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with SD and CV were calculated. pK a and log P and log D Determination The pK a values of ibuprofen were determined using published methods [4, 5] . Ibuprofen was titrated in mixtures of water and ethanol using a GlpKa apparatus (Sirius Analytical Instruments, Forrest Row, UK). The ionic strengths of the solutions were set at 0.15 M with KCl. Yasuda-Shedlovsky plots were used to calculate ‘aqueous’ pK a values [6, 7] . Using octanol as the partition solvent, log P values were also determined by potentiometric titration. The pK a log P program (version 5.1, ACD Ltd.) was also used to predict the distribution coefficients (log D) which were subse- quently compared with the experimentally derived values. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m as de- scribed previously [8] . Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery, obtained following informed consent and with appropri- ate institutional ethical approval was used for skin permeation studies. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [9] . The tissue was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was very carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was immersed in cold water, mounted onto filter paper, and stored in a freezer at –20 ° C until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments (n 6 4) were conducted in Franz dif- fusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, and a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline. The diffusion experiments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. Watkinson /Herkenne /Guy /Hadgraft / Oliveira /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 16 where J represents drug flux, D is the drug diffusion co- efficient, K is the partition coefficient for drug between the stratum corneum and the vehicle, C vehicle is the drug concentration in the vehicle and h is the diffusional path length for the drug. If a cosolvent does not affect membrane barrier prop- erties, its inclusion in the formulation of a weakly acidic drug of the form HX is expected to (1) increase the unionized fraction (HX) of the drug. Since the latter has a higher lipophilicity relative to X – , the overall skin flux, which is proportional to the skin-formulation partition coefficient, K (equation 1) should theoretically be enhanced ; (2) increase drug solubility in the formulation. If the drug concentration is kept constant, this effectively de- creases the degree of saturation and hence should lead to a lower flux. The precise outcome will depend ulti- mately on the nature of the cosolvent, the pH of the formulation and the pK a of the drug. Many non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are or- ganic acids with pK a s ranging from 3 to 5. At the pH of the skin surface (  5), such compounds will be ionized. In the deeper layers of the skin, a less acidic environment is encountered and the degree of ionization of such mol- ecules will therefore increase with consequent effects on solubility and permeation. Ibuprofen is one of the most widely available non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for the management of inflammation and is available in both gel and cream formulations. Although the perme- ation of ibuprofen has been studied from a range of ve- hicles [2] , there has been, to our knowledge, no system- atic investigation into the manner in which formulation components such as cosolvents may influence the ioniza- tion and permeability of ibuprofen into and through the skin. The aim of the present study therefore was to exam- ine the influence of increasing amounts of the cosolvent ethanol on the solubility, ionization and permeation characteristics of ibuprofen through silicone and human skin. As saturated solutions were used throughout this work, a further aim was to investigate the possibility of self-association of ibuprofen in the different cosolvent mixtures. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health- care, Havant, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility of Ibuprofen in Aqueous-Ethanol Mixtures Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were then centrifuged for 15 min at 4,000 g and the supernatant was diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was employed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml  min –1 . The retention time was approximate- ly 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis, and CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Saturated Aqueous Solubility as a Function of pH Excess drug was added to 10 ml of phosphate-citrate buffer [3] at different pH values and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath maintained at 32 ° C. The ionic strength was con- trolled by inclusion of 3 M NaClO 4 in the buffer solutions. Solu- tions were centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was diluted and assayed by UV spectroscopy. Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with SD and CV were calculated. pK a and log P and log D Determination The pK a values of ibuprofen were determined using published methods [4, 5] . Ibuprofen was titrated in mixtures of water and ethanol using a GlpKa apparatus (Sirius Analytical Instruments, Forrest Row, UK). The ionic strengths of the solutions were set at 0.15 M with KCl. Yasuda-Shedlovsky plots were used to calculate ‘aqueous’ pK a values [6, 7] . Using octanol as the partition solvent, log P values were also determined by potentiometric titration. The pK a log P program (version 5.1, ACD Ltd.) was also used to predict the distribution coefficients (log D) which were subse- quently compared with the experimentally derived values. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m as de- scribed previously [8] . Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery, obtained following informed consent and with appropri- ate institutional ethical approval was used for skin permeation studies. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [9] . The tissue was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was very carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was immersed in cold water, mounted onto filter paper, and stored in a freezer at –20 ° C until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments (n 6 4) were conducted in Franz dif- fusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, and a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline. The diffusion experiments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. Effect of Ethanol on Ibuprofen Permeation Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 19 Ethanol-Water Vehicles – Membrane Pretreatment Studies The results of the pretreatment studies showed that the fluxes for the permeation of ibuprofen through sili- cone membrane pretreated with ethanol are not signifi- cantly higher than for the 25: 75 and pure ethanol systems through untreated membrane ( table 4 ). The 75: 25 etha- nol-water formulation has a slightly lower flux (  700 vs.  900  g/cm 2 /h). As noted for the studies where the membrane was not pretreated, relatively low amounts of ethanol can significantly enhance permeation. The parti- tion parameter values for the 100% ethanol vehicle is sig- nificantly higher than for the 25: 75 and 75: 25 vehicles ( table 3 ), suggesting that the major effects of ethanol are to change the solubility properties of ibuprofen in the membrane thus enhancing flux. Permeation Studies in Skin Ibuprofen fluxes across skin are shown in table 5 ; the values were lower than those through silicone membrane ( fig. 4 ). Diffusion across skin increased with the amount of ethanol in the formulation. These effects reflect the ability of ethanol-water mixtures to modify the barrier function of the skin by a number of mechanisms which may include lipid fluidization [16, 17] , lipid disordering [18] , lipid extraction [19] , and modulation of the lipid bar- rier and keratinized protein conformation at low ethanol concentrations [20] . The lower flux values for pure ethanol in human skin versus silicone reflect a direct effect of ethanol on the skin barrier. Ethanol and methanol alter the stratum corneum to a much greater degree than other alcohols [21] and the findings of the present study are in line with earlier ob- servations [22] . In that work, ethanol flux through skin was a function of ethanol volume fraction in the donor solution and dehydration and shrinkage of the stratum corneum was seen after equilibration with pure ethanol. The decrease in oestradiol flux through human skin at ethanol-water ratios higher than 60: 40 (v/v) observed be- fore [15] has also been ascribed to ethanol dehydration effects on the stratum corneum. No major changes in the diffusion or partition param- eters were seen in the formulations containing ethanol ( table 5 ). The partition parameters were all significantly Table 3. Watkinson /Herkenne /Guy /Hadgraft / Oliveira /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 16 where J represents drug flux, D is the drug diffusion co- efficient, K is the partition coefficient for drug between the stratum corneum and the vehicle, C vehicle is the drug concentration in the vehicle and h is the diffusional path length for the drug. If a cosolvent does not affect membrane barrier prop- erties, its inclusion in the formulation of a weakly acidic drug of the form HX is expected to (1) increase the unionized fraction (HX) of the drug. Since the latter has a higher lipophilicity relative to X – , the overall skin flux, which is proportional to the skin-formulation partition coefficient, K (equation 1) should theoretically be enhanced ; (2) increase drug solubility in the formulation. If the drug concentration is kept constant, this effectively de- creases the degree of saturation and hence should lead to a lower flux. The precise outcome will depend ulti- mately on the nature of the cosolvent, the pH of the formulation and the pK a of the drug. Many non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are or- ganic acids with pK a s ranging from 3 to 5. At the pH of the skin surface (  5), such compounds will be ionized. In the deeper layers of the skin, a less acidic environment is encountered and the degree of ionization of such mol- ecules will therefore increase with consequent effects on solubility and permeation. Ibuprofen is one of the most widely available non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for the management of inflammation and is available in both gel and cream formulations. Although the perme- ation of ibuprofen has been studied from a range of ve- hicles [2] , there has been, to our knowledge, no system- atic investigation into the manner in which formulation components such as cosolvents may influence the ioniza- tion and permeability of ibuprofen into and through the skin. The aim of the present study therefore was to exam- ine the influence of increasing amounts of the cosolvent ethanol on the solubility, ionization and permeation characteristics of ibuprofen through silicone and human skin. As saturated solutions were used throughout this work, a further aim was to investigate the possibility of self-association of ibuprofen in the different cosolvent mixtures. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health- care, Havant, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility of Ibuprofen in Aqueous-Ethanol Mixtures Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were then centrifuged for 15 min at 4,000 g and the supernatant was diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was employed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml  min –1 . The retention time was approximate- ly 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis, and CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Saturated Aqueous Solubility as a Function of pH Excess drug was added to 10 ml of phosphate-citrate buffer [3] at different pH values and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath maintained at 32 ° C. The ionic strength was con- trolled by inclusion of 3 M NaClO 4 in the buffer solutions. Solu- tions were centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was diluted and assayed by UV spectroscopy. Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with SD and CV were calculated. pK a and log P and log D Determination The pK a values of ibuprofen were determined using published methods [4, 5] . Ibuprofen was titrated in mixtures of water and ethanol using a GlpKa apparatus (Sirius Analytical Instruments, Forrest Row, UK). The ionic strengths of the solutions were set at 0.15 M with KCl. Yasuda-Shedlovsky plots were used to calculate ‘aqueous’ pK a values [6, 7] . Using octanol as the partition solvent, log P values were also determined by potentiometric titration. The pK a log P program (version 5.1, ACD Ltd.) was also used to predict the distribution coefficients (log D) which were subse- quently compared with the experimentally derived values. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m as de- scribed previously [8] . Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery, obtained following informed consent and with appropri- ate institutional ethical approval was used for skin permeation studies. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [9] . The tissue was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was very carefully peeled away from the dermis. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments (n 6 4) were conducted in Franz dif- fusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, and a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline. Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery, obtained following informed consent and with appropri- ate institutional ethical approval was used for skin permeation studies. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [9] . The tissue was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was very carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was immersed in cold water, mounted onto filter paper, and stored in a freezer at –20 ° C until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments (n 6 4) were conducted in Franz dif- fusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, and a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 . Effect of Ethanol on Ibuprofen Permeation Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 19 Ethanol-Water Vehicles – Membrane Pretreatment Studies The results of the pretreatment studies showed that the fluxes for the permeation of ibuprofen through sili- cone membrane pretreated with ethanol are not signifi- cantly higher than for the 25: 75 and pure ethanol systems through untreated membrane ( table 4 ). The 75: 25 etha- nol-water formulation has a slightly lower flux (  700 vs.  900  g/cm 2 /h). As noted for the studies where the membrane was not pretreated, relatively low amounts of ethanol can significantly enhance permeation. The parti- tion parameter values for the 100% ethanol vehicle is sig- nificantly higher than for the 25: 75 and 75: 25 vehicles ( table 3 ), suggesting that the major effects of ethanol are to change the solubility properties of ibuprofen in the membrane thus enhancing flux. Permeation Studies in Skin Ibuprofen fluxes across skin are shown in table 5 ; the values were lower than those through silicone membrane ( fig. 4 ). Diffusion across skin increased with the amount of ethanol in the formulation. These effects reflect the ability of ethanol-water mixtures to modify the barrier function of the skin by a number of mechanisms which may include lipid fluidization [16, 17] , lipid disordering [18] , lipid extraction [19] , and modulation of the lipid bar- rier and keratinized protein conformation at low ethanol concentrations [20] . The lower flux values for pure ethanol in human skin versus silicone reflect a direct effect of ethanol on the skin barrier. Ethanol and methanol alter the stratum corneum to a much greater degree than other alcohols [21] and the findings of the present study are in line with earlier ob- servations [22] . In that work, ethanol flux through skin was a function of ethanol volume fraction in the donor solution and dehydration and shrinkage of the stratum corneum was seen after equilibration with pure ethanol. The decrease in oestradiol flux through human skin at ethanol-water ratios higher than 60: 40 (v/v) observed be- fore [15] has also been ascribed to ethanol dehydration effects on the stratum corneum.
